text:
NUCLEAR MEDICINE REPORT
WHOLE-BODY BONE SCINTIGRAPHY (TECHNETIUM-99m METHYLENE DIPHOSPHONATE)

INDICATION:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 64-year-old male with known metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (right kidney, pT3bN1M1) with sarcomatoid differentiation (22%), status post cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection. He has been receiving cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily as second-line therapy following progression on ipilimumab/nivolumab. Recent surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis demonstrated stable pulmonary and retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal disease with a new indeterminate 0.5 cm sclerotic focus in the right iliac wing, too small to definitively characterize on cross-sectional imaging. This bone scan is obtained to evaluate for the possibility of osseous metastatic disease.

RADIOPHARMACEUTICAL AND TECHNIQUE:
Intravenous injection of 25.1 mCi (930 MBq) of technetium-99m methylene diphosphonate (Tc-99m MDP) was administered without adverse reaction. Planar whole-body imaging was performed in anterior and posterior projections from vertex to toes at approximately 3 hours post-injection on a dual-head gamma camera. Supplemental spot views of the pelvis, lumbar spine, and bilateral lower extremities were obtained given the clinical question regarding the right iliac bone. SPECT/CT images of the pelvis and lower lumbar spine were additionally acquired and reconstructed for anatomic correlation of areas of interest.

COMPARISON:
Prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (most recent, approximately 6 months post-cabozantinib initiation).

FINDINGS:

SKULL AND CERVICAL SPINE:
No focal areas of abnormal radiotracer uptake are identified in the calvarium, skull base, or cervical spine. Symmetric and homogeneous tracer distribution in the visualized cranial and upper axial skeleton.

THORACIC SKELETON:
Symmetric radiotracer uptake throughout the ribs bilaterally. No discrete focal abnormality to suggest cortical disruption or blastic/lytic metastatic lesion. Mild, bilaterally symmetric uptake at multiple costo-chondral junctions is noted, a nonspecific but common degenerative finding. The thoracic vertebral bodies demonstrate uniform tracer activity without focal photopenic or hyperactive lesion. No sternal or clavicular abnormality.

LUMBAR SPINE:
Uniform radiotracer uptake in the lumbar vertebral bodies from L1 through L5. No focal lesion identified. There is mild, diffusely increased uptake at the L4-L5 and L5-S1 facet joints bilaterally, consistent with degenerative facet arthropathy and in keeping with the patient's age. This pattern is symmetric and does not suggest metastatic involvement.

PELVIS AND SACRUM:
The sacrum demonstrates homogeneous, symmetric radiotracer uptake. The sacroiliac joints show mildly increased but symmetric activity bilaterally, consistent with degenerative changes.

Right iliac wing: On the planar images, there is a subtle focus of mildly increased radiotracer uptake projecting over the right iliac wing, corresponding in location to the 0.5 cm sclerotic focus identified on the prior CT. This area of uptake is discrete but low-grade in intensity -- it does not demonstrate the brisk, intense radiotracer avidity that is typically associated with aggressive osteoblastic metastatic disease. The degree of uptake is only modestly above background and symmetric regional bone marrow activity. On SPECT/CT correlation, this focus localizes to a small area of cortical sclerosis in the right iliac wing without associated soft tissue mass, periosteal reaction, or cortical destruction. The morphologic and scintigraphic appearance is most consistent with a benign process such as a bone island (enostosis) or reactive/degenerative sclerosis, rather than a metastatic deposit. There is no photopenic center within the focus that would suggest central necrosis characteristic of a large metastasis.

Left iliac wing, ischium, and pubic rami bilaterally: No focal abnormality. Bilateral acetabular regions show symmetric uptake without discrete lesion.

FEMORA AND LOWER EXTREMITIES:
Normal radiotracer distribution throughout the bilateral proximal and mid femora. The femoral heads and necks are symmetric in uptake. The bilateral knees show mild periarticular uptake consistent with mild degenerative joint disease. Tibiae, fibulae, and feet demonstrate uniform tracer distribution. No focal lesion suggestive of osseous metastasis in the visualized lower extremities.

UPPER EXTREMITIES:
Symmetric radiotracer distribution in the bilateral humeri, radii, ulnae, and hands. No focal abnormality identified.

KIDNEY AND SOFT TISSUES:
As expected in the setting of prior right nephrectomy, there is absent tracer excretion from the right renal fossa. The left kidney demonstrates normal tracer uptake and excretion. Background soft tissue activity is within normal limits for the administered dose and timing. There is no focal soft tissue uptake to suggest extraosseous metastatic disease or sites of dystrophic calcification.

BLADDER:
The urinary bladder contains radiotracer representing physiologic renal excretion of the radiopharmaceutical. No pelvic soft tissue abnormality is scintigraphically evident.

IMPRESSION:

1. SINGLE FOCUS OF MILDLY INCREASED UPTAKE IN THE RIGHT ILIAC WING, corresponding to the 0.5 cm sclerotic lesion noted on recent CT. The scintigraphic characteristics of this focus -- low-grade intensity, absence of a photopenic center, and absence of cortical destruction or periosteal reaction on SPECT/CT correlation -- are most in keeping with a benign etiology such as a bone island or focal reactive sclerosis. There is no convincing scintigraphic evidence of an aggressive or metastatic process at this site. This appearance is not typical of renal cell carcinoma osseous metastasis, which tends to be lytic or photopenic rather than densely sclerotic.

2. NO ADDITIONAL FOCAL AREAS OF ABNORMAL RADIOTRACER UPTAKE are identified in the axial or appendicular skeleton to suggest osseous metastatic disease elsewhere. In particular, there are no photopenic lesions, no areas of intense focal hyperactivity in a pattern consistent with osteoblastic metastases, and no evidence of the "superscan" pattern.

3. DEGENERATIVE CHANGES involving the lumbar facet joints, bilateral sacroiliac joints, and bilateral knees are noted as an incidental finding consistent with the patient's age and prior history.

4. ABSENT RIGHT RENAL UPTAKE consistent with prior right nephrectomy.

Overall, this whole-body bone scan does not demonstrate convincing evidence of osseous metastatic involvement. The indeterminate right iliac sclerotic focus identified on the prior CT most likely represents a benign bone island or focal reactive change; continued surveillance with cross-sectional imaging is appropriate if there is any interval change in appearance.
